# OpenSpec Enables Detecting Unexpected Modifications from Proteomics Data Generated by Orbitrap Astral Mass Spectrometer

## Citation

> [!cite] Reference
> **Title**: OpenSpec Enables Detecting Unexpected Modifications from Proteomics Data Generated by Orbitrap Astral Mass Spectrometer
> **Authors**: Min Tang, Zhao Sun, Chengpin Shen, Xiaoqing Wang, Xinhang Hou, Xiang Liu, Qiushi Wei, Ao Zhang, Yuzhuo Gu, Jiayuan Zeng, Zekun Cai, Zhenchao Tang, Yang Fu, Jing-Hua Yang, Chao Liu
> **Year**: 2025
> **Journal**: Analytical Chemistry
> **DOI**: 10.1021/acs.analchem.5c03055

## Abstract

The Orbitrap Astral mass spectrometer features outstanding speed, resolution, and sensitivity, making data-independent acquisition (DIA) the preferred method for deep profiling in shotgun proteomics. However, as for data generated by an Orbitrap Astral mass spectrometer, the current search engines cannot detect unexpected modifications, which are novel in biology and chemistry systems. Here we present OpenSpec, a computational workflow specifically designed for comprehensive identification of unexpected modifications from Astral-DIA data sets. The workflow incorporates a Transformer-based precursor-fragment grouping model to deconvolute DIA data to generate DDA-like pseudo-MS/MS spectra, achieving a DDA-based open search strategy on Astral-DIA data. We evaluated OpenSpec through a benchmarking study with synthetic peptides emulating diverse modification patterns and complemented by systematic comparison between DIA and DDA acquisition modes on identical samples. We investigated unexpected modifications of cysteine across various sample pretreatment conditions.

## Key Points

- OpenSpec utilizes a Transformer-based model to group precursor and fragment features, effectively deconvoluting highly complex Astral-DIA data into DDA-like pseudo-MS/MS spectra.
- The pipeline applies an open search strategy using pFind3 on the pseudo-MS/MS spectra to discover unexpected modifications, which are then integrated into a hybrid spectral library for final DIA-NN searching.
- OpenSpec outperforms traditional workflows like library-free DIA-NN and DIA-Umpire in retaining modification information and detecting modified peptides, as validated on synthetic datasets and paired DIA-DDA experimental data.

## Methods

### Sample Preparation
- Method used: [[Data-Independent Acquisition]] (DIA), [[Data-Dependent Acquisition]] (DDA), iodoacetamide (IAA) alkylation vs. non-alkylation protocols
- Cell type: [[293T]] cell lysate spiked with synthetic peptides
- Number of cells: N/A (bulk lysates)

### Data Analysis
- Software: [[OpenSpec]], [[DIA-NN]], [[pFind3]], [[DIA-Umpire]], [[PepPre]], [[FragPipe]]
- Downstream: [[Spectral Deconvolution]], [[Open Search]], [[Hybrid Spectral Library]] generation

## Results

### Main Findings
1. OpenSpec successfully retrieved all 20 synthetic modified peptides, whereas DIA-NN (in library-free mode, even with specific variable modification settings) and DIA-Umpire missed several due to failure to generate high-quality pseudo-spectra or score them confidently.
2. In a large-scale DIA-DDA paired dataset, OpenSpec reliably identified unexpected modifications (e.g., Formyl[S], Deamidated[N]) in DIA mode with retention times and fragment ion matching highly consistent with DDA mode.
3. Analysis of sample pretreatments revealed that OpenSpec accurately detected Disulfide[C] as the most abundant cysteine modification when IAA alkylation was omitted, and Carbamidomethyl[C] when IAA was used, proving its capability to capture condition-specific unexpected modifications.

### Figures

| Figure | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| Fig 1 | Workflow of OpenSpec consisting of three steps: generating pseudo-MS/MS spectra using a Transformer model, constructing a hybrid spectral library with DDA open search, and final identification using DIA software. |
| Fig 2 | Identification results of the synthetic peptides dataset, comparing the number of peptides identified by OpenSpec, DIA-NN, and DIA-Umpire, and showing an example pseudo-MS/MS spectrum generated by OpenSpec. |
| Fig 3 | Identification results comparing the DIA-DDA paired data, showing the frequency of modified peptides in both modes and a spectral match comparison for a specific modified peptide. |
| Fig 4 | Frequencies of modified peptides identified from datasets with and without IAA treatment, alongside a pseudo-MS/MS spectrum for a disulfide-modified peptide found in the non-IAA sample. |

## Discussion

### Strengths
- Enables open search on highly complex, convoluted Astral-DIA data by generating stable and reliable DDA-like pseudo-MS/MS spectra.
- Can be integrated with existing mainstream search engines (e.g., DIA-NN) without requiring additional clinical sample consumption.
- Highly sensitive and robust in discovering "dark matter" modifications that are normally missed by restricted DIA search spaces.

### Limitations
- Currently optimized and focused primarily on DIA data generated by the Orbitrap Astral mass spectrometer.

### Future Work
- Expand the application of OpenSpec to other mass spectrometry instruments and software platforms.
- Apply the workflow to specific clinical cohort samples to help researchers explore the role of unexpected modifications in the regulation of disease development.
